tldr: Switch from a single curated Lucide set to Iconify's universal API spanning 200k+ icons across many sets — useful when you need access to multiple icon families without bundling each one.
why_switch: Access to 200k+ icons across 150+ sets via one API
On-demand loading avoids bundling every icon variant up front
Framework components for React, Vue, Svelte, plus a Figma plugin
Mix and match brand logos, mdi, heroicons, etc. without separate packages
Open-source core if you want to self-host the icon API
what_to_export: List of every Lucide icon name in use across the codebase
Design tokens mapping semantic names to icon components
Wrapper component(s) currently rendering Lucide icons
List of additional sets you actually want from Iconify
export_method: Grep for Lucide imports (lucide-react, lucide-vue-next, etc.) and collect the full set of icon names. Map each Lucide icon to its Iconify equivalent — Lucide icons are mirrored in Iconify under the lucide prefix (e.g. lucide:home), but always confirm the exact prefix and name in the official Iconify icon catalog before relying on it.
import_method: Install the Iconify component for your framework per its official docs (e.g. @iconify/react, @iconify/vue) and render icons as <Icon icon="lucide:home" /> or equivalent. Update your wrapper to accept a string name instead of a component. Decide whether to use Iconify's hosted API or to bundle commonly used icons offline — both are documented options. Keep accessibility props applied at the wrapper level.

concept_mapping.note: Component-based vs string-based; refactor wrapper API.

concept_mapping.target: Many sets via prefixes (mdi:, heroicons:, ph:)
concept_mapping.note: Pick prefixes deliberately to avoid style drift.
Choose strategy per Iconify docs based on perf needs.
Behavior depends on the chosen icon set.
Verify each set's license before use.

gotchas: Switching from imports to string names is a real refactor across the codebase
Each Iconify set has its own license — review before using brand or paid sets
Runtime icon fetching adds a small latency hit unless you bundle offline
Mixing visual styles across sets can break design consistency
SSR setups need correct Iconify configuration to avoid hydration mismatches
If you only need one icon family, sticking with Lucide is usually simpler

email.subject: Evaluating a switch from Lucide to Iconify
email.body:
Hey team,

I'm scoping a move from Lucide to Iconify. The win is access to 200k+ icons across many sets through one API — useful as we add brand logos, product-specific glyphs, and material symbols without bundling separate libraries. The cost is a refactor from named imports to string-based icon names plus a careful look at on-demand loading behavior.

Plan: enumerate every Lucide icon in use, map each to its Iconify lucide:* equivalent, refactor the wrapper, and decide on runtime API vs offline bundling per Iconify's official docs. Estimated effort: 3-8 hours.

Open to staying on Lucide if folks don't see value in the broader catalog.

Thanks
